data.title: Allspring Common Stock Fund: Q1 2026 Top Contributors And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Allspring Common Stock Fund Q1 2026 Commentary.

The Allspring Common Stock Fund underperformed the Russell 2500 Index during the period. Performance was mainly driven by negative stock selection, with sector allocation also having a slight negative effect on overall results. Health care was the most significant detractor, led by weakness within life sciences tools and services, while consumer discretionary and IT also weighed on relative performance. Financials was the largest contributor, supported by strong stock selection within capital markets and select banking names.

The broader market backdrop was mixed during the period. Strength in cyclical areas such as energy and industrials reflected improving optimism around industrial activity and higher commodity prices, while smaller- and mid-cap growth-oriented sectors faced pressure from elevated interest rates, volatility in IT, and heightened scrutiny of earnings visibility. Within this environment, the portfolio's exposure to select financials provided relative support but was outweighed by declines in several individual holdings.

Stocks Contributing to Relative Performance Include:

Littelfuse, Inc. (LFUS), designs and manufactures circuit protection, sensing, and power control products serving electronics, automotive, and industrial end markets. LFUS delivered strong performance in the quarter as results and guidance exceeded expectations, supported by stronger bookings (B/B > 1 and bookings up >20% year over year) and solid backlog entering 2026, which drove improved operating leverage. Performance was led by the electronics segment, where growth tied to data center, grid, utilities, and renewable applications more than offset softness in residential HVAC, commercial vehicles, and certain areas of power semiconductors. Management highlighted increasing encouragement around early signs of industrial recovery and continued outgrowth in automotive while previewing portfolio actions ahead of a planned May analyst day, including deeper integration of recent acquisitions and a sharper focus on higher-value semiconductor applications. Data center and AI-related demand were also cited as areas of increasing strategic importance as power architectures grow more complex.

Regal Rexnord Corp. (RRX) engineers and manufactures factory automation sub-systems, industrial powertrain solutions, automation and mechanical power transmission components, electric motors and electronic controls, air moving products, and specialty electrical components and systems. RRX performed strongly in the first quarter, primarily on account of 1) a quarterly report that featured particularly strong orders owing to budding demand for RRX electrical products sold into the data center market, which drives a substantial revision to 2027 growth expectations, and 2) two >50 Institute for Supply Management Manufacturing Purchasing Manager's Index prints following three years of sub-50 conditions. We consider RRX to be an attractive security offering exposure to improving prospects for growth in U.S. manufacturing investment, with an expanding side opportunity for growing its small data center electrical business.

Westlake Corp. (WLK) is a global diversified chemical and building products company that engages in the manufacturing and supplying of PVC, caustic soda, polyethylene, and various building products. WLK outperformed in the first quarter due to the effects on global chemical markets from the conflict in Iran, which has had an extreme impact on the supply of global chemical products. Various competing global chemical producers have declared unforeseen circumstances or had their input costs materially inflate, while WLK's operations and costs have been largely unaffected, as its facilities are located in the U.S. and thus have access to unaffected U.S. natural gas supplies. The situation highlights WLK's core advantage pertaining to its place on the global cost curve and security of operations. WLK's separate building products company brings exposure to eventual improvements off of depressed levels in home improvement spending and construction activity.

Kirby Corp. (KEX) provides inland and coastal marine transportation services for bulk liquid products and distributes equipment and services to energy and industrial customers. KEX performed well in the quarter as marine transportation results improved through the period, with inland barge utilization recovering meaningfully by quarter-end and margins coming in better than expected. While pricing softened modestly during the quarter, management characterized the decline as temporary, citing favorable renewal activity and continued capacity discipline. KEX has also benefited from power generation needs from data centers in their engines segment and should benefit from any commodity production increases in the U.S. to offset shut-in capacity globally related to the Iran conflict.

Stocks Detracting from Relative Performance Include:

Azenta, Inc. (AZTA), provides biological and chemical sample management, genomics, and laboratory automation solutions to research and clinical customers, generating revenue through service contracts and consumables. AZTA underperformed the benchmark during the quarter as profitability weakened, with adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) margins pressured by underused lab capacity, Automated Stores remediation costs, and softer demand in portions of Sample Management Solutions. Organic revenue in Sample Management declined modestly, reflecting continued challenges in the Automated Stores and Cryo businesses, while earnings fell year over year despite modest topline growth. AZTA completed the acquisition of UK Biocentre, which is expected to dilute margins in fiscal 2026 but expands European biorepository capabilities and supports longer-term growth objectives.

Herc Holdings, Inc. (HRI), provides equipment rental solutions to construction, industrial, and infrastructure customers, generating revenue primarily from fleet rentals and related services. HRI performed poorly during the quarter as investor attention centered on elevated leverage and near-term execution risk despite solid fourth-quarter results and rental demand. Management spent the period focused on integrating the H&E Equipment Services acquisition, including branch conversions and sales territory optimization that added operational complexity early in the year. The company also implemented a new regional operating structure to support future scale and efficiency gains, though benefits have yet to be reflected in sentiment. Overall, the quarter highlighted steady underlying demand but ongoing integration and balance sheet considerations that weighed on shares.

Planet Fitness, Inc. (PLNT), operates a franchised and company-owned fitness center network, generating revenue from memberships, franchise royalties, and equipment sales. Shares fell after cautious full-year guidance signaled slower sales and earnings growth, despite strong quarterly performance. While revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and earnings exceeded expectations, management highlighted near-term headwinds, including weaker join trends early in the year, higher churn in January, and a more back-half-weighted club-opening schedule. Additional pressure came from lower expected equipment sales and incremental cost headwinds that tempered the outlook for fiscal 2026. The quarter reflected steady brand demand and continued unit growth, but investor sentiment shifted toward normalization after several years of outsized growth.

Revolve Group, Inc. (RVLV), operates an online fashion platform selling apparel, footwear, and beauty products to a global, digitally native customer base. RVLV had lackluster performance during the quarter as investor reaction centered on higher planned marketing spend and a more cautious margin outlook, despite solid revenue growth and improving customer engagement. Results showed accelerating demand, supported by broad-based category growth and increased active customer additions, but near-term profitability expectations were tempered as the company leaned into brand investment. Management also continued to expand its own brand portfolio and beauty offering, which contributed to growth but weighed modestly on average order values. Overall, the quarter reflected strong topline execution while near-term expense discipline remained the primary focus for investors.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

For additional detailed fund characteristics data, please visit our web site, Allspring Global Investments: Investment Management & Services, self identify on the landing page, then click Investments and Mutual Funds via the top navigation.

Portfolio holdings and characteristics are subject to change and may have changed since the date specified. The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to purchase or sell a particular security.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Russell 2500™ Index measures the performance of the small-to mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. It is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index and includes approximately 2,500 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market capitalization and current index membership. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The S&P 500 Index consists of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity, and industry group representation. It is a market-value-weighted index with each stock's weight in the index proportionate to its market value. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Institute of Supply Management (ISM) Manufacturing Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) is an index of the prevailing direction of economic trends in the United States manufacturing and service sectors. It consists of a diffusion index that summarizes whether market conditions, as viewed by purchasing managers, are expanding, staying the same, or contracting. The purpose of the PMI is to provide information about current and future business conditions to company decision makers, analysts, and investors. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Diversification does not ensure or guarantee better performance and cannot eliminate the risk of investment losses.

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition of Terms:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks:

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Smaller company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than those of larger companies. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled mid-cap blend funds, the Common Stock Fund received 1 star among 365 funds, 1 star among 341 funds, and 1 star among 263 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. The Morningstar Rating is for the Institutional Class only; other classes may have different performance characteristics.

The Morningstar absolute ranking is based on the fund's total return rank relative to all funds that have the same category for the same time period. Morningstar rankings do not include the effect of sales charges. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

† The inception date of the Institutional Class was July 30, 2010. Historical performance shown for the Institutional Class prior to its inception reflects the performance of Class A and includes the higher expenses applicable to Class A. If these expenses had not been included, returns would be higher.

The views expressed and any forward-looking statements are as of March 31, 2026, and are those of the fund managers and/or Allspring Global Investments. Read it carefully before investing.

These firms include but are not limited to Allspring Global Investments, LLC, and Allspring Funds Management, LLC. data.title: April's 5 Dividend Growth Stocks With Yields Up To 8.16%
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

For some background on this monthly publication, here is my view on dividend growth stocks:

Dividend growth stocks aren't always the most exciting investments out there. They often aren't grabbing the headlines, and they aren't the stocks running up hundreds of percentages in a year. In fact, they are often some of the least exciting stocks. And that is precisely their strongest selling point. With such a vast world of dividend growth stocks available out there, it is important to screen through to see if there are any worthwhile investments to explore.

They are stocks that provide growing wealth over time to income investors. Dividend growers are often larger (not always), more financially stable companies that can pay out reliable cash flows to investors. Some are slower growers than others. Some are going to be cyclical that require a strong economy. Some are going to be secular, which doesn't generally rely on a more robust economy.

Dividend growth can promote share price appreciation. Of course, that is if these companies are growing their earnings to support such dividend growth in the first place. Trust me. There are yield traps out there - I've owned a few that I'm not particularly proud of.

I like to think of investing in dividend stocks as a perpetual loan of sorts. Essentially, every dividend is a repayment of your original capital. Eventually, holding long enough, you have the position "paid off." It is all returned back into your pocket from that point forward.

All of this being said, it is important to understand my approach to dividend stocks and why screening dividend stocks can be important for income investors. As with any initial screening, this is just an initial dive - more due diligence would be necessary before pulling the trigger.

The Parameters For Screening

I'll be using some handy features that Seeking Alpha provides right here on their website for this screen. In particular, I will be screening utilizing their quant grades in dividend safety, dividend growth and dividend consistency.

Dividend Safety is relatively self-explanatory. These will be stocks that SA quants show reasonable safety compared to the rest of their various sectors. The grade considers many different factors, but earnings payout ratios, debt and free cash flow are among these. This category will be stocks with A+ to B- ratings.

For the dividend growth category, we have factors such as the CAGR of various periods relative to other stocks in the same sector. Additionally, the quants also look at earnings, revenue and EBITDA growth. As we will see, this doesn't mean that every stock with a higher grade has the growth we are looking for. This just factors in that the dividend has grown or earnings are growing to support possible dividend growth. For these, the grades will also be A+ through B- ratings.

Finally, for dividend consistency, we want stocks that will be paying reliable dividends for us for a very long time. In particular, hopefully, they are raising yearly, though that isn't an explicit requirement. We will also include stocks with a general uptrend in dividend payments, which means there could have been periods where they paused increases for a year or two.

After looking at those factors alone, we are left with 309 stocks at this time, which is up just a bit from last month's 302. However, the highest yield is also down from over 8% for the top-ranked previously.

I'll link the screen here, though it is a dynamic list that constantly updates regularly. When viewing this article, there could be more or less when going to the link. As prices fluctuate, the dividend yields can also change from the time of writing as well.

From there, I wanted to narrow down the list a lot more. I then sorted the list by TTM dividend yield, from highest to lowest. Since these will be safer dividend stocks in the first place, screening for those among the higher payers shouldn't hurt. Though there are always exceptions, after all, this is simply an initial screening, and I try to note those that I feel aren't as safe as the algorithms are suggesting.

As is usually the case, we have a number of names that are repeats from month to month. In our highlights today, we'll be skipping over T. Rowe Price (TROW) and Kimberly-Clark (KMB) since we covered them within the last month.

With that, it leaves us with RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ), VICI Properties (VICI), Best Buy Co (BBY), NNN REIT (NNN) and The Clorox Company (CLX).

#1 RLJ Lodging Trust 8.16%

This is a name that has shown up for us many times now over the last year; it is also a name that I consider relatively riskier to consider. While the algorithm gives it a high grade on dividend safety, I'd be a bit more cautious. As a hotel and resort-focused REIT, this name is going to be quite sensitive to overall economic conditions. They are also focused on higher-end premium rooms with a limited portfolio of 92 hotels. With an enterprise value of only around $3.4 billion, this is quite a small REIT as well.

During Covid they ended up slashing their dividend meaningfully and held it at a token level for a number of years. They then started to raise it aggressively over the last couple of years.

This is on the back of weakening AFFO, and it doesn't look like analysts are too optimistic looking forward either.

With a net debt to EBITDA of around 6x, they are pushing the elevated levels even for REITs. If earnings don't improve, this then starts to look even worse.

On a more optimistic note, though, the AFFO payout ratio based on 2026 estimates comes in at ~44%. That is on the very low side relative to most REITs, leaving them plenty of room and potentially time to hopefully come up with a turnaround plan while leaving the dividend intact. Further, it comes in with a dirt-cheap forward P/AFFO of 5.4x.

Overall, I would consider this a more speculative consideration for investors.

#2 VICI Properties 6.44% Yield

VICI continues to be a name that I find attractively valued, but one that is continually pressured given the challenges it is currently facing. This is a Las Vegas casino-focused REIT, but they have been expanding into other types of experiential properties. It is primarily the decline in tourism to the strip that has been an overhang. While the REIT simply collects the rent checks, mostly sheltering it from the economic ebbs and flows, too much pressure on tenants can lead to trouble for the landlord.

They are highly concentrated in primarily Caesars and MGM Resorts, representing over 70% of their annualized rent. So despite some diversification efforts, they still command an overwhelming amount of exposure.

Still, I have faith that VICI will remain fine, considering they had already weathered through Covid. Albeit a rather brief but ultimately type of worst-case scenario that played out. Even though growth is expected to slow down for VICI, analysts are still expecting growth going forward, despite these challenges as well.

That should continue to help support the growing payout that they've been delivering to investors.

I'm currently long shares of VICI from writing puts previously at $32.50, and I have another short put trade at the $27.50 strike price. Suffice it to say, I find this name attractive to consider for investors who don't mind a bit of a riskier REIT play.

#3 Best Buy Co 5.91% Yield

This is only the second time that BBY has made the discussion portion of our monthly piece. The name had been facing earnings pressure as EPS had stagnated for a number of years after electronics demand waned after covid. They also have to face ever-increasing competitive pressures of online shopping from the likes of Amazon (AMZN).

Though optimistically, analysts are starting to expect a turnaround and earnings growth to resume once again. However, even in the more optimistic scenario of earnings growth starting back up, the company isn't expected to hit those levels again over the next five years.

They've also continued to increase their dividend to investors annually, at the token 1 penny increase to keep the streak alive. This brings them up to 21 years of consecutive dividend increases.

Based on this fiscal year's estimates, the payout ratio comes in at just shy of 60%. Should they experience the growth analysts estimate, then this starts to ease payout pressures even further—even if we assume a penny raise in each of the coming years.

At its current forward P/E of ~10x, investors aren't too enthusiastic, as this is a relatively cheap valuation for this name.

Should they meet the estimates of analysts or even exceed them, I could see BBY being a great bargain here as the share price likely does head significantly higher in the coming years. Thus, the nearly 6% yield now makes it look quite enticing while waiting for that potential turnaround.

#4 NNN REIT 5.56% Yield

This is another name that I currently own in the REIT space, and I continue to find it attractive here. In fact, I had been writing puts as a way to potentially grow my position at the $40 strike level. We are at a price where that could start making sense again to continue with writing puts—potentially even just adding some shares outright.

As a retail-focused REIT, this name has faced pressures from a higher rate environment. Given the fact that it is expected that the Fed is likely not going to be cutting rates further this year, and risk-free Treasury rates have been rising, we are once again seeing those pressures play out. There has even been some chatter that the Fed may be pushed into a position to possibly raise its benchmark rate as well on the back of surging oil prices.

That's to be determined, but that has all put pressure on NNN. When rates started heading higher in 2022/2023, we can see that the multiple for this name started facing pressure quite meaningfully. More recently, we are seeing that again and at 12x forward P/AFFO, I think it is looking quite attractive.

They have a long history of raising their payout each year, going on 35 years now. With that sort of commitment, I don't see that ending anytime soon.

Given the AFFO payout ratio of 67.6%, they aren't in a position of straining to support their dividend either. That's on top of the fact that earnings are expected to continue to trend higher. It's certainly a slow grower, but this isn't one of those names that is expected to be overly exciting either. It offers a decent yield that grows over the long term.

For an income investor, I believe that NNN continues to remain a strong candidate for adding at this time.

#5 The Clorox Company 4.88% Yield

CLX is a household name that probably needs little introduction, but it is a name that has never made the discussion portion of our pieces either.

Over the last year, the share price is down over 30%, and that has pushed its yield higher—though they've also been raising their payout consistently as well. In fact, this is a name that is almost a dividend king. A company that has been able to raise its payout for 50+ years, and CLX is at year 48.

While I'm sure they'll achieve the dividend king crown and keep pushing higher, it isn't without some risks to consider. This is a consume staples stock, but its earnings have been quite volatile over the years. Interestingly, similar to BBY, they had a strong earnings growth tied to Covid, but then that collapsed after.

Now, heading into fiscal 2026, earnings are expected to come under significant pressure again. On a brighter note, they are supposed to rebound and start trending higher once again heading into 2027. That said, if they come in at the $5.92, that would push their payout ratio to 84%. A very elevated level for a company like this.

Revenue growth has been limited, with them noting they expect net sales to be down 6-10% this fiscal year. They also highlighted that EPS is expected to decrease from 9-14% year-over-year, with a "negative impact of about 90 cents related to the reversal of the impact from incremental shipments associated with the ERP transition..." Which helps to highlight at least partially why earnings are expected to drop so sharply this year but then rebound, as this is a one-time event.

Despite the sharp decline in the share price, the forward P/E comes in at 15.3x.

Historically, that is incredibly low and would otherwise be a cheap valuation. However, I'm not sure investors will care, given it is not necessarily a dirt-cheap valuation on an absolute basis. It also looks like it enjoyed a few years of outsized premium multiple around Covid, which would have dragged its whole average even higher.

That said, should we get any sort of signs that they are on their way to returning to an earnings growth trajectory and sales bottom out, I think this could be another strong candidate as a turnaround play. Though it could likely be a few quarters before we start to get any idea if they are likely to be successful or not.

data.title: Allspring Common Stock Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The Allspring Common Stock Fund underperformed the Russell 2500 Index during the period.
  • Stock selection in health care and consumer discretionary sectors and underweight allocation to energy detracted from relative performance.
  • Selection in financials benefit performance in the quarter.

Market review

U.S. equity markets declined during the first quarter of 2026, reflecting a challenging macroeconomic and policy environment. Elevated valuations, shifting expectations around the path of monetary policy, and renewed geopolitical tensions weighed on sentiment. Oil prices jumped to their highest levels in three years, while prices for soft commodities also increased. Meanwhile, gold and silver declined from their recent peaks, as rising yields and shifting rate expectations appeared to reduce the appeal of precious metals, highlighting how investors are reacting to changing inflation and policy trends. The S&P 500 Index fell 4.3% for the quarter, with most of the weakness occurring early amid inflation concerns, questions around the timing of Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts, and higher energy prices. While geopolitical tensions added to volatility by pressuring energy markets and inflation expectations, investors remained focused on earnings fundamentals rather than a broader economic disruption.

A late-quarter rebound helped stabilize markets but was not enough to offset earlier losses. Despite weaker headline results, underlying market conditions were more balanced than index performance suggested. Declines were largely concentrated among a narrow group of mega-cap stocks, particularly within growth-oriented segments sensitive to higher rates, while leadership shifted toward areas with more durable earnings and cash flows. Energy led the market, supported by higher commodity prices and supply-side disruptions, alongside strength in utilities, materials, and select industrials. Within information technology (IT), performance varied, as companies tied to artificial intelligence (AI)-related infrastructure held up better than software and other more rate-sensitive growth segments. While consensus earnings expectations for 2026 remained constructive, elevated valuations limited broader index gains.

Small-and mid-capitalization stocks were relatively more resilient. The Russell 2500 Index returned 2.04% for the quarter, modestly outperforming large-cap equities. More balanced sector exposure and reduced concentration helped support results, with value-oriented and higher-quality segments outperforming large-cap growth, though tighter financial conditions continued to challenge more leveraged companies. Investor preferences increasingly favored businesses with strong cash flows, solid balance sheets, and greater insulation from rising input costs.

Various competing global chemical producers have declared unforeseen circumstances or had their input costs materially inflate, while WLK's operations and costs have been largely unaffected, as its facilities are located in the U.S. and thus have access to unaffected U.S. natural gas supplies. The situation highlights WLK's core advantage pertaining to its place on the global cost curve and security of operations. WLK's separate building products company brings exposure to eventual improvements off of depressed levels in home improvement spending and construction activity.

Kirby Corp. (KEX) provides inland and coastal marine transportation services for bulk liquid products and distributes equipment and services to energy and industrial customers. KEX performed well in the quarter as marine transportation results improved through the period, with inland barge utilization recovering meaningfully by quarter-end and margins coming in better than expected. While pricing softened modestly during the quarter, management characterized the decline as temporary, citing favorable renewal activity and continued capacity discipline. KEX has also benefited from power generation needs from data centers in their engines segment and should benefit from any commodity production increases in the U.S. to offset shut-in capacity globally related to the Iran conflict.

Stocks Detracting from Relative Performance Include:

Azenta, Inc. (AZTA), provides biological and chemical sample management, genomics, and laboratory automation solutions to research and clinical customers, generating revenue through service contracts and consumables. AZTA underperformed the benchmark during the quarter as profitability weakened, with adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) margins pressured by underused lab capacity, Automated Stores remediation costs, and softer demand in portions of Sample Management Solutions. Organic revenue in Sample Management declined modestly, reflecting continued challenges in the Automated Stores and Cryo businesses, while earnings fell year over year despite modest topline growth. AZTA completed the acquisition of UK Biocentre, which is expected to dilute margins in fiscal 2026 but expands European biorepository capabilities and supports longer-term growth objectives.

Herc Holdings, Inc. (HRI), provides equipment rental solutions to construction, industrial, and infrastructure customers, generating revenue primarily from fleet rentals and related services. HRI performed poorly during the quarter as investor attention centered on elevated leverage and near-term execution risk despite solid fourth-quarter results and rental demand. Management spent the period focused on integrating the H&E Equipment Services acquisition, including branch conversions and sales territory optimization that added operational complexity early in the year. The company also implemented a new regional operating structure to support future scale and efficiency gains, though benefits have yet to be reflected in sentiment. Overall, the quarter highlighted steady underlying demand but ongoing integration and balance sheet considerations that weighed on shares.

Planet Fitness, Inc. (PLNT), operates a franchised and company-owned fitness center network, generating revenue from memberships, franchise royalties, and equipment sales. Shares fell after cautious full-year guidance signaled slower sales and earnings growth, despite strong quarterly performance. While revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and earnings exceeded expectations, management highlighted near-term headwinds, including weaker join trends early in the year, higher churn in January, and a more back-half-weighted club-opening schedule. Additional pressure came from lower expected equipment sales and incremental cost headwinds that tempered the outlook for fiscal 2026. The quarter reflected steady brand demand and continued unit growth, but investor sentiment shifted toward normalization after several years of outsized growth.

Revolve Group, Inc. (RVLV), operates an online fashion platform selling apparel, footwear, and beauty products to a global, digitally native customer base. RVLV had lackluster performance during the quarter as investor reaction centered on higher planned marketing spend and a more cautious margin outlook, despite solid revenue growth and improving customer engagement. Results showed accelerating demand, supported by broad-based category growth and increased active customer additions, but near-term profitability expectations were tempered as the company leaned into brand investment. Management also continued to expand its own brand portfolio and beauty offering, which contributed to growth but weighed modestly on average order values. Overall, the quarter reflected strong topline execution while near-term expense discipline remained the primary focus for investors.

Fund positioning and outlook

Markets are likely to remain sensitive to macroeconomic developments, including the path of Fed policy, inflation trends, and evolving geopolitical and trade dynamics. While expectations for eventual rate cuts remain supportive, uncertainty around timing and policy outcomes may sustain volatility, particularly within small-and mid-cap equities. Against this backdrop, we remain steadfast in our commitment to valuation discipline and broad-based diversification, ensuring portfolios are not overexposed to any one theme or factor. By focusing on durable businesses and maintaining a selective, nimble approach, we believe the strategy is well positioned to navigate near-term volatility and capture long-term opportunities as market leadership broadens and value regains prominence.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Russell 2500™ Index measures the performance of the small-to mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. It is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index and includes approximately 2,500 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market capitalization and current index membership. The S&P 500 Index consists of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity, and industry group representation. It is a market-value-weighted index with each stock's weight in the index proportionate to its market value. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Institute of Supply Management (ISM) Manufacturing Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) is an index of the prevailing direction of economic trends in the United States manufacturing and service sectors. It consists of a diffusion index that summarizes whether market conditions, as viewed by purchasing managers, are expanding, staying the same, or contracting. The purpose of the PMI is to provide information about current and future business conditions to company decision makers, analysts, and investors. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Diversification does not ensure or guarantee better performance and cannot eliminate the risk of investment losses.

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition of Terms:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks:

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Smaller company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than those of larger companies. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

data.title: Stock Market At Record High As Iran Crisis Deepens
data.description:
The US stock market closed at a record high on Friday (Apr. 17), but a few days ago is ancient history when the firehose of war news can reshape investor sentiment by the hour. Right on cue, the trading week ahead has plenty of fresh, shape-shifting headlines to process and decide if last week’s optimism revival still resonates.

Among the highlights over the weekend: Iran reversed its earlier agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz to restart energy exports, with hardliners in the country asserting control and insisting it will remain shut as long as the US blockade continues. A US Navy ship fired on an Iranian cargo ship, violating America’s blockade, later seizing the vessel. Iran vowed to retaliate. Meanwhile, Vice-President Vance is headed to Pakistan again for peace talks, but Tehran says it has no plans for further talks with the US.

What this means for markets is anyone’s guess, but equities begin the week from a position of strength. The S&P 500 Index’s recovery set a record for speed, but the drama may not be over just yet. The key challenge is that while stocks priced in an effective end to the war, it’s still not obvious that a quick solution to the supply-energy shock is imminent.

“We had the most violent day in the strait on Saturday that we’ve had since the beginning of this crisis, and things don’t seem to be getting any better,” Rory Johnston, founder of Commodity Context, tells CNBC. “While we keep getting these sell-offs and it keeps seeming like we’re about to finally get that football — Lucy pulls it away — and we’re back to where we started. The strait still isn’t flowing, and 13 million barrels a day of production remains shut-in. We’re losing it every single day this goes on.”

As for the resiliency in the stock market through Friday’s close, much of the hefty lifting comes from technology stocks. Using a set of ETFs to track the sectors that comprise the S&P 500 highlights that tech (XLK) is the leading source of gains by far when measured since the war’s start through the end of last week. XLK is up more than 11% during the conflict, far above the S&P 500’s 3.8% rise since Feb. 28. The rest of the sectors are split with a mix of gains and losses.

Upbeat earnings news is a key factor for the stock market’s overall resiliency. FactSet reports: “For Q1 2026 (with 10% of S&P 500 companies reporting actual results), 88% of S&P 500 companies have reported a positive EPS surprise and 84% of S&P 500 companies have reported a positive revenue surprise.”

In terms of performance, however, tech is still doing the hefty lifting, largely thanks to optimism, overbaked or not, related to the business opportunities in artificial intelligence (AI).

Therein lies a familiar question for investors that’s taken on greater relevance: Can bullish sentiment fueled by AI keep the party going in what could be a protracted battle over reopening the strait?

One reason that the bulls are still answering “yes” relates to expectations of the disinflationary effect of AI on the economy. Citing that “many companies are talking about efficiency gains from AI,” the head of Northern Trust’s $1.4 trillion asset management unit predicts: “If even a portion of those [gains] actually materialize on an economy-wide basis, it could be one of the biggest positive supply shocks we’ve ever seen,” Mike Hunstad tells the FT. “You can’t ignore that.”

By that reasoning, the “massively disinflationary” outlook he thinks is possible leaves room for the Fed to stand pat on monetary policy until it’s clear how AI-driven productivity gains evolve.

“AI has the potential to be massively disinflationary,” Hunstad advises.

Perhaps, but whether that’s enough to keep stocks rallying in the short term is another question entirely.

data.title: CFTR.PR.A: An 9.5% Preferred Stock IPO From Cantor Fitzgerald Income Trust
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

This is the next article in our series targeting new fixed-income securities on the exchange. This time, our attention will be focused on Cantor Fitzgerald Income Trust Inc.'s new addition to the exchange - the 9.50% Series A Cumulative Preferred Stock (CFTR.PR.A). As usual in this type of analysis, we'll go through the issuer's fundamentals to check out whether we want exposure to its preferred equity at all. The issuer is not listed on any of the major exchanges, so this step in our analysis is more important than usual. Next, we'll continue with the sector's peers comparison to assess the investment qualities of CFTR-A in a relative value analysis.

The New Issue

The prospectus contains all relevant information regarding the new preferred stock. Here, we will focus on only the metrics that are most important for our analysis.

CFTR-A pays an 9.50% annual cumulative dividend in $0.5938 quarterly distributions. Dividends on the new preferred shares will accrue from April 8, 2026, starting distributions on July 31, 2026. This means the first dividend payment will be irregular in size and will amount to approximately $0.7455. As the issuer is a REIT, these preferred distributions are not eligible for the preferential tax rate. CFTR-A is a tiny issue of only 0.8 million shares with a liquidation preference of $25 each, so Cantor Fitzgerald Income Trust's gross proceeds from the new security are $20 million. The new perpetual preferred stock issue was not scored by any of the three big credit rating agencies at the date of its IPO. It has no stated maturity date and may be subject to early redemption by the Trust on or after April 8, 2031.

As the issue is just listed, its price chart looks like this:

Currently, CFTR-A is priced at $24.91 with a current yield of 9.53%, and the yield to call is determined to be 10.00% using the XIRR calculation:

This is what the stock's YTC curve looks like right now:

Company basics and performance

Launched operation in 2017 and headquartered in New York, Cantor Fitzgerald Income Trust is a publicly registered, but not publicly traded real estate investment trust, externally managed by Cantor Fitzgerald Income Advisors, LLC. The Trust is a separate, affiliated entity of Cantor Fitzgerald, L.P., designed to deliver tax-advantaged distributions to its shareholders. To achieve this goal, CF Income Trust employs a strategy of investing at least 80% of its portfolio into office, industrial, multifamily, retail, or other real estate properties:

As of the end of February 2026, CF Income Trust's portfolio is worth of $1.1 billion across 43 properties, with a footprint across the U.S.

CF Income Trust has several classes of common shares, with pretty identical NAVs per share, and distributions in the 5% range. The 5% of NAV per share target distribution rate was set as of the beginning of 2026:

The company provides its annualized total return performance, too, which does not look very impressive to me:

Whether you pick the year-to-date, 3-year average, or annualized since inception metric, the numbers are too low. An additional note we can make here is that each of the share classes will distribute more than their total return performance on whichever period of average you pick. If the portfolio continues the pace of its past performance, a considerable amount of the distribution will have to be in the form of return of capital, directly associated with the depletion of NAV per share.

Capital Structure

Below is the capital structure of CF Income Trust as of the end of 2025. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is also presented for comparison in the short term.

As of the end of December 2025, CF Income Trust had approximately $595.6 million in debt, senior to the common equity of $470.3 million. The debt-to-equity ratio was approximately 1.27. The $20 million of CFTR-A will fit in between these two in terms of seniority.

Dividends

With 12.1 million common shares outstanding and a 5% on NAV annual target distribution rate, we can calculate a forward annual common share distribution of $12.1 million. The TTM total interest expenses were $28.8 million, and CFTR-A's preferred dividend will amount to $1.9 million per year. This means that the common shares distribution can cover only 39% of the fixed-income expenses of the Trust. Above, we pointed out that part of this expected common shares dividend will be in the form of a return of capital, so the cushion provided by the income-generating portfolio of the Trust is even thinner.

CFTR-A Credit Quality Assessment

CFTR-A is a perpetuity issued with an 9.50% nominal yield, or 462 basis points above the 30-year Treasury yields (US30Y) at the time of the IPO.

Looking at the average Single-B rated U.S. corporate debt OAS and comparing it to the CFTR-A's IPO risk premium, we can see that, according to the underwriters, the new preferred stock is priced as a security somewhere below Single-B in credit quality:

This is an assessment that is putting the new preferred among the securities that bear a substantial investment risk:

Peers Comparison

In the comparison, I've included unrated perpetual preferred shares issued by property REITs.

And the metrics:

In this comparison group, CFTR-A is among the preferreds, offering a current yield higher than the average. As usual, the higher yield comes as compensation for the higher risk credit-wise. On a downshift in the long-term benchmark yield, c offers almost no capital appreciation potential, unlike discounted peers from this set. This is something that should also be taken into consideration by potential investors.

Use Of Proceeds

We will contribute these net proceeds to our operating partnership in exchange for preferred units of our operating partnership which have economic interests that are substantially similar to the designations, preferences and other rights of the Series A Preferred Stock. We, acting through our operating partnership, will use the net proceeds from this contribution to acquire our target assets in a manner consistent with our investment strategies and investment guidelines described in this prospectus, refinance, repurchase or repay certain existing indebtedness of ours and our subsidiaries, including amounts outstanding under our credit facility, and for general corporate purposes.

Source: CFTR-A Prospectus

Special Optional Redemption

Upon the occurrence of a Change of Control during a continuing Delisting Event, each holder of Series A Preferred Stock will have the right, unless, prior to the Conversion Date, we have provided or provide notice of our election to redeem the shares of Series A Preferred Stock, to convert some or all of the Series A Preferred Stock held by the holder into a number of shares of our Class I common stock (hereinafter sometimes referred to as “Class I shares”) or, if any class of our common stock is listed on a national securities exchange, of such class of common stock, per share of Series A Preferred Stock, which is equal to the lesser of:

• the quotient obtained by dividing (i) the sum of the $25.00 liquidation preference per share of Series A Preferred Stock to be converted plus an amount equal to all dividends accrued and unpaid (whether or not authorized or declared), if any, on the Series A Preferred Stock to, but not including, the Conversion Date (as defined herein) (unless the Conversion Date is after a dividend record date and prior to the corresponding dividend payment date, in which case no additional amount for the accrued and unpaid dividend will be included in this sum), by (ii) the Common Stock Price (as defined herein); and

• 2.47647, referred to herein as the “Share Cap,” subject to certain adjustments;

subject, in each case, to provisions for the receipt of alternative consideration as described in this prospectus.

Source: CFTR-A Prospectus

Conclusion

I have no current investment interest in CFTR-A. The preferred is a tiny issue from a non-listed externally managed company. The Trust's portfolio performance provides poor coverage for the company's fixed-income securities. The underwriters have put the new preferred among the securities bearing substantial investment risk. On top of this, the lot of peer preferreds offer better appreciation potential, should the long-term end of the yield curve downshift. I'll pass on this one.

data.title: Regulatory Breakthroughs Unlocked For U.S. Nuclear
data.description:
By Steffan Szumowski

The Nuclear Regulatory Commission’s (NRC) arduous licensing process remains the most frequently cited barrier to rapidly iterating and deploying nuclear energy in the United States. Traditional frameworks — built around large, light-water reactor designs — have required newer technologies to undergo lengthy reviews and seek extensive exemptions.

Clearing the Path to Commercialization

Now, recent regulatory actions under the Department of Energy (DOE) and the NRC are proving revolutionary in their ability to speed up the licensing process while maintaining rigorous safety standards. These changes directly address the backlog of construction applications expected over the next few years. They will also help clear the path to commercialization.

Improved licensing pathways represent a significant tailwind to the public and private companies developing new reactors. This also includes the firms that will play a supporting role by providing key components and services. The VettaFi Nuclear Renaissance Index (NUKZX) includes the nuclear equipment manufacturers, service providers, and construction firms that are expected to see higher demand for their offerings as new reactors are built. An improved regulatory landscape was a much-needed catalyst for new nuclear development.

DOE Pathways Deliver Early Wins

On April 6, 2026, Antares Nuclear (private) became the first company to receive DOE approval of its Documented Safety Analysis (DSA) for its Mark-0 microreactor under the DOE’s Reactor Pilot Program (RPP). The approval validates the reactor’s final design and safety case. Antares is now preparing to turn its reactor on for the first time and attain criticality. Criticality refers to a sustained chain reaction of uranium atoms splitting inside the core.

DOE leadership has remarked that the approval of a DSA is equivalent to an NRC license. Considering Antares started its licensing process under the DOE only eight months ago, the fact that the company has already received approval to commence startup preparation is truly groundbreaking.

NRC Modernizes Rules to Clear the Path

The NRC finalized a new path for reactor licensing last month. This new pathway, Part 53, marks the first new reactor licensing framework in decades:

  • Originally, reactor license applicants had only one option, called Part 50. This path involves a two-step process where the reactor developer first gets permission to construct the reactor. After the reactor is built, the company applies for a license to turn on and operate the plant.
  • Part 52 was eventually approved. It allowed the construction and operation permit reviews to be combined into one step for a more streamlined process.
  • Because Part 50 and 52 tailor specifically to the large reactors produced by companies like Westinghouse (CCJ), the industry required an additional path. Companies with more exotic designs have had to make endless exception requests and perform multiple studies and safety evaluations that aren’t applicable to their reactors.

The new, risk-informed, technology-inclusive regulation creates an alternative pathway tailored for advanced reactors. This also includes non-light-water designs and small modular reactors (SMRs). Under Part 53, applicants gain greater flexibility to demonstrate safety through performance-based analyses rather than the prescriptive rules designed for older technologies. The new framework reduces unnecessary duplication in reviews, allows licensing in stages, and establishes clearer timelines.

The NRC has also been hard at work this year issuing multiple new rules. This includes streamlined environmental reviews, standardization of environmental studies, additional regulatory pathways for microreactors, and more structured and streamlined public hearing processes. The environmental regulation changes alone are projected to save as much as 6,500 man-hours of preparation and review per reactor license.

Benefits for Key Reactor Developers & Manufacturers

These regulatory advancements promise meaningful speed and cost advantages to several leading companies within NUKZX:

  • Cameco (CCJ), through its ownership stake in Westinghouse, is anticipated to progress through licensing much faster with the AP1000 reactor.
  • GE Vernova (GEV) stands to benefit significantly with its BWRX-300 SMR design.
  • Advanced reactor developers such as Oklo (OKLO) are also well positioned. The ability to reference prior DOE test data and the technology-inclusive rules under Part 53 should substantially ease their path to commercialization.
  • Heavy equipment manufacturers, including BWX Technologies (BWXT) and Curtiss-Wright (CW), are providing equipment and fuel to companies including Antares.
  • These also represent opportunities, offering investors a unique means of accessing the potential success of private companies in the nuclear sector.

Licensing timelines that once stretched for years can now benefit from risk-informed frameworks, prior-authorization credits, and targeted microreactor pathways. The changes support both large-scale advanced reactor projects, SMRs, and factory-built microreactors suited for data centers, remote power, and industrial applications.

NUKZX includes companies across the nuclear value chain, from fuel to utilities. NUKZX is the underlying index for the Range Nuclear Renaissance Index ETF (NUKZ).

data.title: Texas Roadhouse: Too Expensive, Look To Add Closer To Fair Value (Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I previously covered Texas Roadhouse, Inc. (TXRH) in July 2024, where I gave the stock a Buy rating based on strong fundamentals. Although the fundamentals remain relatively strong, with a high level of free cash flow amid their expanding restaurants, TXRH has underperformed the market by only achieving a 1.28% total return over this time. However, over the last 3 years on an annualized basis, the total return for TXRH has only recently fallen below the broader market.

Given TXRH's ability to navigate a difficult economic environment successfully, their strong fundamentals, and management's focus on the business, I believe TXRH presents a strong value diversification in a portfolio of riskier assets at the right price. Given a discounted cash flow model of valuation based on expected earnings per share and current levels of free cash flow, TXRH trades at a premium despite strong fundamentals.

Previous Coverage

I last covered Texas Roadhouse in July 2024, where I discussed their fundamentals, fair value price target, and compared their stock performance to other popular fast casual restaurants such as The Cheesecake Factory Incorporated (CAKE), Brinker International, Inc. (EAT) and Yum China Holdings, Inc. (YUMC) with their performance over the last decade. Updating that comparison below. TXRH has still significantly outperformed peers over the last decade. However, EAT recently had two strong years while TXRH has stalled, which shows the intense competition in this space. Further, I described how TXRH has consistently grown their revenue by an annualized 12% a year, maintained a positive ROIC to WACC ratio, and had a high return on equity. Although they displayed a solid fundamental picture, investors have remained mixed over the last couple of years, and the stock has remained stagnant.

Earnings Outline

Highlighted in their 2025 full-year earnings report, they grew total revenue to $5.9 billion, announced a 1.9% price increase to their menu items starting in Q2, and mentioned completion of the digital kitchen rollout. Of note, same-store sales increased by 4.9% in 2025, which was driven by 2.8% growth in foot traffic. Peers in the fast casual chain, such as CAVA, have been reporting lower levels of same-store sales growth than TXRH. Looking ahead to their next earnings, investors should pay attention to same-store sales growth and consumer traffic given the current macroeconomic conditions. Further, the company is focused on effectively managing increasing costs, such as pressure from beef price fluctuations, while increasing returns to investors with double digit dividend growth. Looking forward, investors should focus on any changes to their dividend policies and how increases in commodity prices impact earnings.

Current Fundamentals

Currently, TXRH trades at a sector premium with a D- valuation rating on Seeking Alpha. The P/E ratio is 27.11 over the trailing twelve months and 26.27 on a forward basis, EV/EBITDA is 17.21 on a trailing twelve-month basis and 16.31 on a forward basis, and the price-to-sales ratio is 1.86 on a trailing twelve month basis and 1.66 on a forward basis. As with most of the typical valuation metrics, TXRH is trading at a premium compared to the sector medians; however, these values are in line with the 5-year average for TXRH.

As mentioned in my previous article, TXRH has strong fundamentals. They have maintained a return on invested capital, a return on assets, and a return on equity all over 10% on average over the last decade, excluding the pandemic between 2020 and 2022 (see chart below).

Cash Flow

On a profitability basis, TXRH receives a B+ rating from Seeking Alpha, which is generated from their strong cash flows from operations over the trailing twelve months, which far exceeds the sector median. Currently, TXRH provides $5.053 of free cash flow per share, with a free cash flow margin of 5.72% and a gross profit margin of 12.42%. Although margins have remained stable in the last ten years, free cash flow has increased.

Dividend

Contributing to TXRH's total return is their consistently growing dividend. During volatile market environments, dividend paying stocks can provide a cushion to the volatility of individual portfolios and may help with overall portfolio diversification. TXRH currently pays $3.00 per share with a forward yield of 1.82% and has maintained a growing dividend for the last 14 years. There's also plenty of room for future dividend growth with their current payout ratio of just over 44%.

Risks

There are risks to this thesis that investors should consider. Given the volatile macroeconomic environment, the consumer may be at risk for a slowdown. Although consumers have remained resilient in the short-term, if spending declines, consumer discretionary companies like fast-casual restaurants could see an impact due to decreased consumer spending. This would ultimately lead to an impact on TXRH's financial outlook. Further, as TXRH is primarily focused on offering beef on their menus, changes in the cost of beef may have an impact on the company moving forward. There is some concern that beef prices may continue to climb in the near future; however, management has done a good job managing increases in costs so far in difficult economic environments.

Finally, another risk to this thesis is that TXRH continues to underperform the broader market due to being near their 5-year average valuation, which is a risk investors should take seriously and consider only adding during downtrends. Evaluating an expected return for TXRH given their general risk measured by beta, you should expect to achieve at least an 11% total return, which TXRH has not done in the last couple of years.

Valuation

Analysts believe that earnings per share will grow to $7.63 per share on average by the end of 2027, with a total revenue estimate of $7.15B. Given these metrics, TXRH would have a P/E ratio of 21.64 and a price/sales ratio of 1.52. Using a discounted cash flow model based on earnings per share and given 2026's EPS estimate of $6.28, $7.63 per share would represent a roughly 21.5% annualized growth rate given a typical 10% discount.

Given the underperformance of the company recently, but also accounting for their continued plans for future restaurant openings, I'm assuming EPS will grow on average by 8% for the next 10 years, with 3% terminal growth thereafter. Further, I used the same discounted cash flow model to analyze their free cash flow level of $5.05, but instead of 8% growth, I used a growth rate of 12.66%, which is consistent with their current return on total capital. By doing this, and using the CAPM required rate of return of 11% as a discount rate, investors should look to buy in the fair value range of $109-$122. Compared to the current price, TXRH trades at a 30% premium to estimated fair value.

Conclusion

In conclusion, TXRH presents an opportunity to diversify into a strong dividend grower at the right price that has weathered difficult economic environments, such as the pandemic, effectively. I expect management to continue to focus on the underlying business and maintain growing cash flow for their investors. Although the stock is still trading at a premium valuation and has been stagnant over the last couple of years, the underlying business remains strong with strong fundamentals. I believe TXRH could add diversification in the consumer discretionary sector while providing a modest dividend during a volatile market environment.

Given the premium to fair value, investors may find better opportunities in the market, but would benefit by keeping this on a watch list to add during market downturns. Investors should perform their own research and consult financial professionals before making any investment decisions.

data.title: AST SpaceMobile: The Pullback I Was Waiting For
data.description:
The problem here is not that one particular satellite failed. Launch delays are expected in any space venture; however, the market sometimes tends to treat them as anomalies. What makes AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) notable is the transition from the speculative business model to a business model that requires execution.

This is my explanation of the subsequent decline in the price after the BlueBird 7 episode. The problem here is not about the failure of the satellite, which performed normally when launched into orbit. This malfunction relates to the launch vehicle's poor performance.

However, this is a different case since the main issue now is not about the technology of the company. The key aspect here is that at this moment the company is becoming more dependent on external parties, especially on launches.

I am upgrading my earlier rating from Hold to Buy on the basis that the sell-off was an opportunity for resetting expectations rather than the thesis. The stock is now valued based on execution risk rather than perfect execution, as previously. The combination of technology, demand confirmation, and a solid balance sheet makes for an excellent risk-reward proposition.

Execution Reset, Not a Collapse

To make this clear, I was quite skeptical about investing in AST SpaceMobile prior to the news of the launch failure. The reason for this view was simple: the company's setup was simply getting ahead of itself. To that end, while I would not say that this is a bargain price range, the current decline represents much better buying grounds for me.

If I had to define the current pullback, I would rather call it a repricing of execution-related risks. ASTS had begun underperforming well before the launch date, which told me that there was an expectation for an event with binary results. Naturally, the launch malfunctioning confirmed the negative outcome.

The problem with the selloff is that the loss of BlueBird 7 means a lot more than just losing one more satellite. Whereas the company could come up with several versions of their product already, this specific piece was a component of Block 2 satellites that were much more powerful, at least one order of magnitude, than the previous versions. Loss of even one such piece may lead to some severe delays.

The malfunction of BlueBird 7 was caused by a failure in the launch rocket and had nothing to do with any deficiencies in the technological solutions provided by AST. It was the poor performance of the second stage that doomed this satellite to the orbit from which it would never be able to escape. This means that the satellite is now de-orbited.

While I don’t think it shatters the thesis statement of the project, I think that this problem should not be taken lightly either. The potential value creation by AST lies in its ability to rapidly develop a functional constellation, so this is very important here. One missing satellite will not break things, but one missed launch window might.

Ultimately, this failure highlights the critical nature of AST’s launch windows, meaning that the company will have far less margin for error moving forward and greater risk associated with the future launches. In other words, while the thesis is still intact, it definitely became a bit fragile for that matter.

Is the Target 2026 Deployment Still Achievable?

My assumption about the feasibility of the 2026 deployment is that it is possible, but with more difficulty than before. In the case of the failed BB7 satellite launch, the program had to be executed almost flawlessly. Today, however, it requires putting up around 45 satellites into orbit by 2026 due to fast production enabling multiple satellites to be manufactured per month.

The thing that keeps me up at night at this point is the margin. This approach is extremely dependent upon timing and synchronization. It’ll work, but there is no room for any sort of error. Each subsequent issue will add up and may result in failing to reach the deadline. The bottom line is that the goal is still feasible but with different circumstances. Before, it looked achievable with good implementation, while now it requires perfect execution.

Valuation Reset, Not Thesis Break

What makes ASTS interesting, in my view, is not that it has suddenly become cheap by traditional standards; it clearly hasn’t. The stock has a forward EV/Sales multiple of about 140, making it seem very pricey from any traditional valuation metric. However, I don’t believe this to be the complete story.

Ultimately, what the valuation stands for, at its core, is the confidence in the success of the transition from the stage of infrastructure construction (pre-revenue stage) to developing a scalable and profitable platform. Consensus forecasts suggest the company will be generating about ~$180 million in 2026, reaching the level of ~$1.8 billion in 2028 with more than $3 billion expected longer-term. As a result, the valuation multiples contract quite sharply towards the single-digit Price/Sales mark rather fast, which becomes much more reasonable.

Where I am betting at this moment is that the market might be overly discounting the execution risk after such a price decline. The company enjoys a solid balance sheet, no dilution risk in sight for now, and demand already proven through partnerships/contract backlog. This time around, however, I am not buying the stock just because it is statistically cheap. I am buying it because of the reset in risk/reward.

Launch Risk Will Decide the Upside

As I have already mentioned, it is very possible that AST SpaceMobile manages to get its satellites launched again. If this happens without further problems or disruptions, we should see the company's stock recovering rapidly. However, should it continue to experience troubles, I would need to revisit my thesis.

First of all, this would require further disruptions in the network deployment process. Second, the company should be unable to provide a concrete plan for future activities that will help the business to achieve its 2026 goal without any further problems. Otherwise, it will mean that AST is likely to suffer losses of some magnitude.

Bottom Line

I am now turning bullish on ASTS since I view this drop we have seen as an expectation reset and not a change in the fundamentals. The technology is working, there is proof that the demand exists, and there is no issue with the company’s balance sheet. What has changed is the attitude and the stock price.

data.title: Black Stone Minerals: May See Modest Benefits From Improved Long-Term Oil Prices
data.description:
Black Stone Minerals (BSM) has seen its unit price go down by over 10% since I last looked at it (in late February 2026). It is heavily hedged on both oil and natural gas in 2026, so near-term commodity price changes have little direct effect on its 2026 distributable cash flow. For example, a $40 increase in the average price of oil during 2026 would only increase Black Stone's 2026 distributable cash flow by three cents per unit, independent of any changes to its production levels.

However, the Middle East conflict has resulted in an increase in my long-term (after 2026) oil price expectations. That has increased from $70 to $75 (for WTI oil), translating into a small increase in my estimate of Black Stone's value.

I now estimate Black Stone's value at $16.00 per unit (up from $15.50 per unit) and consider it modestly undervalued now. This is based on long-term (after 2026) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Updated 2026 Outlook

The strip for 2026 is now around $80 WTI oil and $3.60 NYMEX natural gas. Black Stone is very heavily hedged though (roughly 88% on natural gas and 85% on oil), so there is only a minor direct impact on its free cash flow from changes in commodity prices.

At the current strip, Black Stone is projected to generate $420 million in revenues, inclusive of negative $32 million in 2026 hedge value.
